content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 2, 1992


William R. Galstan
City Attorney
P. O. Box 130
Antioch, CA  94509-0504

                                        Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                            Our File No. I-92-343

Dear Mr. Galstan:

     This letter is in response to your request for informal
assistance¹ regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of
Antioch Councilmember Cathryn Freitas under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").²

                              QUESTIONS

     1.  Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Councilmember Freitas to disqualify herself from voting to approve
the city's participation in the joint area-wide municipal national
pollutant discharge elimination system program?

     2.  Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Councilmember Freitas to disqualify herself from voting to fund
the city's financial participation in the program by approving the
levy of assessments or fees?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1 and 2.  Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, Councilmember Freitas may participate in decisions of the


_________________________
     ¹ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

     ² Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-92-343
Page 2

city council to approve the city's participation in the program
and to fund the city's participation in the program by approving
the levy of assessments or fees. Her participation would not be
prohibited unless the decisions will be akin to decisions to hire,
fire, promote, demote, or discipline her spouse or to set a salary
for her spouse which is different from salaries paid to other
employees of her spouse's agency in the same job classification or
position. (Regulation 18702.1(c)(1).)

FACTS

Councilmember Freitas' spouse is an employee of the Contra
Costa County Public Works Department. He has been designated as
the program manager of a joint area-wide municipal national
pollutant discharge elimination system ("NPDES") program. The
program is federally mandated and is enforced through the regional
water quality control boards. Local government agencies are
required to submit applications to the regional board and obtain
NPDES permits. Generally, the function of the NPDES program is to
identify sources of pollutants which may find their way into local
storm drain systems and, ultimately, to protected waters, in this
instance the San Francisco Bay and its delta system. The goal is
to reduce pollutants through elimination of their discharge into
the storm drain system, to educate the public, and to take other
steps such as increased street sweeping.

Contra Costa County, the 18 cities within the county, and the
Contra Costa County Flood Control District have formed a joint
area-wide NPDES agency. This agency will conduct studies, apply
for permits, and carry out some or all of the necessary programs.
Funds for these payments will come from the members' general
operating funds, or they could be raised by a variety of methods,
including the imposition of special assessments, fees, or charges.

Mr. Freitas' sole duties with the county public works
department are to serve as the program manager of the joint NPDES
program. The costs to be paid by the members to the county
include reimbursement of Mr. Freitas' salary and benefits, as well
as other administrative overhead and expenses. The total of these
administrative costs is $189,412. Of this sum, $168,000 will be
paid by the Contra Costa County Flood Control District, an agency
of the county. Its contribution is specifically designated to
reimburse the salary and administrative expenses of the program
manager and his administrative support staff. The remaining sum
of $22,000 will be paid by the other members, including the city
of Antioch. The total cost of the next phase of the project is
$084,000. The city of Antioch's share of this cost is 7.72%, or
$70,715.

It is anticipated that Mr. Freitas' entire salary and
benefits will be reimbursed by the Flood Control District's
contribution of $168,000. However, the remaining $22,000 in
administrative expenses will be borne by the other members, of
which the City of Antioch's share would be $1,698.40.

Our File No. I-92-343
Page 3


          Councilmember Freitas has authorized you to request our
advice on her behalf.

                              ANALYSIS

          Section 87100 of the Act prohibits public officials from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.  A "public official" is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency.  Councilmember Freitas is a public official.

          Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official, or on a member of his or her immediate family, or on:

               Any source of income, other than gifts and
          other than loans by a commercial lending
          institution in the regular course of business on
          terms available to the public without regard to
          official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
          dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
          received by or promised to the public official
          within 12 months prior to the time when the
          decision is made.

                         Section 87103(c).

          You have advised us that Councilmember Freitas' spouse is an
employee of the Contra Costa County Public Works Department.  As
the manager of the NPDES program, his salary will be paid by the
county.³  Members of the NPDES agency, a joint powers agency
created to administer the program, will reimburse the county on a
pro rata basis for this expenditure.⁴  It is expected that the
Contra Costa County Flood Control District, an agency of the
county, will contribute $168,000 to the program.  This
contribution is specifically designated to reimburse the salary
and administrative expenses of the program manager and his
administrative support staff.

          Councilmember Freitas has a community property interest in
the income of her spouse.  (Section 82030; Jones Advice Letter,
No. A-92-153.)  Accordingly, one half of her spouse's income is

_______________

³  For purposes of our discussion, we assume that Mr. Freitas'
salary will exceed $500 within a twelve-month period.

⁴  The City of Antioch is a member of the NPDES joint powers
agency.  All members of the NPDES are public agencies.

Our File No. I-92-343
Page 4

attributed to her. Thus, the County of Contra Costa is a source
of income to Councilmember Freitas in excess of $250 within a
twelve-month period.

However, the Act excludes from the definition of "income" any
salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a
state, local, or federal government agency. (Section 82030(b)(2);
Fatland Advice Letter, No. I-89-419.) Accordingly, Councilmember
Freitas' share of her husband's income from the county does not
create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions affecting
the county. (Battersby Advice Letter, No. A-90-300.)

A conflict of interest may still exist where the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of Councilmember Freitas or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing by $250, irrespective of the source of
the income or decreases. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4); Insurance
Advice Letter, No. I-89-142.) Thus, this provision contemplates
even increases and decreases to income received from governmental
agencies as potentially disqualifying economic interests.

Notwithstanding the provisions of Regulation 18702.1(a)(4),
Regulation 18702.1(c)(1) provides that an official does not have
to disqualify himself or herself from a governmental decision if
the decision only affects the salary, per diem, or reimbursement
for expenses the official or his or her spouse receives from a
state or local government agency. Accordingly, even if the
pending decisions will affect Mr. Freitas' salary, Councilmember
Freitas may participate freely in decisions to approve the city's
participation in the program. She may also participate freely in
decisions to fund the city's financial participation in the
program by approving the levy of assessments or fees.

However, the exception in Regulation 18702.1(c)(1) does not
apply to "decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or discipline
an official's spouse, or to set a salary for an official's spouse
which is different from salaries paid to other employees of the
spouse's agency in the same job classification or position."
Thus, Councilmember Freitas may not participate in decisions which
would result in a promotion or demotion for her spouse, or in
decisions to hire, fire, discipline or set a salary for her spouse
which is different from salaries paid to other employees of her
spouse's agency in the same job classification or position.

You have stated in your request for advice that Mr. Freitas
has been designated as the program manager of the joint NPDES
program. One of the pending decisions before Councilmember
Freitas is a vote to approve the city's participation in the
program. If this vote would affect Mr. Freitas' status as manager
of the program, Councilmember Freitas would be disqualified from
participating in the decision. Similarly, if a vote to fund the
city's contribution to the program would result in a promotion or
demotion for Mr. Freitas, or if Mr. Freitas were to be fired from
his position as manager of the program as a result of the pending

Our File No. I-92-343
Page 5

decisions, Councilmember Freitas would be disqualified from
participating in the decisions.

We have previously advised that the elimination of a city
department which employs the spouse of a public official would be
construed as "firing" the employee pursuant to Regulation
18702.1(c). (Schreiber Advice Letter, No. A-88-459.)
Accordingly, if failure of the city of Antioch to participate in
the program would result in the elimination of the program, the
pending decisions would be construed as "firing" Mr. Freitas.
Thus, Councilmember Freitas would be disqualified from
participating in the decisions.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.5

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Bianca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:bb

5
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
